key_points: A polynomial of degree n can have at most n real zeroes
The number of zeroes equals the number of x-axis intersections of its graph
Repeated zeroes occur when the graph touches the x-axis
Relationship exists between zeroes and coefficients of quadratic polynomials
Remainder and Factor Theorems help analyse polynomials algebraically
examples: If zeroes of x² − 7x + 10 are 2 and 5, then sum of zeroes = 7
Graph touching x-axis at one point shows repeated zero
If p(1) = 0, then (x − 1) is a factor of p(x)
formulas: Remainder Theorem → p(a) gives remainder on division by (x − a)
exam_tips: Use degree to predict number of zeroes before solving
Touching vs cutting the x-axis is crucial in graph-based questions
Forming polynomial from given zeroes is a common HOTS question
Always link algebraic zeroes with graphical behaviour
